chunk_summary: This section outlines epinephrine access plans and school readiness for IgE-mediated food allergies, emphasizing safety protocols, dosing guidelines, and caregiver/school training to prevent delays in emergency management.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of epinephrine access as a systemic safety measure for IgE-mediated food allergies.
Learn proper intramuscular epinephrine dosing and administration techniques for emergencies.
Develop school readiness plans, including allergen documentation and emergency protocols.
Recognize the limitations and safeguards of resource-limited epinephrine delivery methods.
key_facts: Standard intramuscular epinephrine dosing is 0.01 mg/kg of 1 mg/mL into the anterolateral thigh.
Epinephrine autoinjectors are the first choice, with device-specific training and renewal checks.
Pre-filled syringe programs should have strict safeguards, including 3-month replacement intervals and caregiver teach-back.
Epinephrine is not indicated for mild symptoms like isolated oral itch or a few hives without progression.
School readiness plans must include allergen documentation, symptom recognition, epinephrine storage details, and emergency calling procedures.
reference_quote: The minimum safety bundle is a written action plan plus skills training using demonstration and teach-back, repeated at follow-up because technique decays. (Source: Anaphylaxis Management in OPD, IAP Action Plan 2026)
document_key_topics: Epinephrine administration and dosing for food allergies
School readiness and emergency action plans
Safety protocols for resource-limited epinephrine access
